content.problemStatement:
You are given two integers LLL and RRR. Find the smallest non-negative integer NNN such that
N % L>N % (L+1)>…>N % (R−1)>N % R .N \,\%\, L \gt N \,\%\, (L + 1) \gt \ldots \gt N \,\%\, (R - 1) \gt N \,\%\, R \,.N%L>N%(L+1)>…>N%(R−1)>N%R.

Here, %\%% is the modulo operator, so A % BA \,\%\, BA%B is the remainder of AAA after division by BBB. For example, 11 % 3=211 \,\%\, 3 = 211%3=2.
constraints: When a solution exists, the minimal N does not exceed 10¹⁸
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers L and R (1 ≤ L < R ≤ 10⁶).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the smallest non‑negative integer N that satisfies N%L > N%(L+1) > … > N%R, or -1 if no such N exists.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For (4,6): 6 ≤ 2·4‑1, so answer is R = 6. For (1,2): 2 > 2·1‑1, condition fails, thus -1.
All three pairs satisfy R ≤ 2·L‑1, so the minimal N equals R in each case.
Here 9 > 2·4‑1 = 7, the required inequality cannot hold, so no N exists.

solution.approach: For a decreasing sequence of remainders of length (R‑L+1) we need at least (R‑L) steps to go from the first remainder down to 0, therefore N%L ≥ R‑L. Since N%L ≤ L‑1, a necessary condition is L‑1 ≥ R‑L, i.e. R ≤ 2·L‑1. This condition is also sufficient: choosing N = R gives N%k = R%k = R‑k for every k in [L, R] because R < 2·L ≤ k+L, thus the remainders form the strictly decreasing sequence R‑L, R‑(L+1), …, 0. Consequently, the smallest possible N is simply R when the condition holds, otherwise no solution exists.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
